text: What hit me most about Obsession is that a day later, I still cannot stop thinking about it. And I think that's because the film is operating on a much smarter level than its premise initially suggests. This is the kind of setup that could have gone wrong in so many obvious ways. It could have become broad "hashtag cinema," where the creepy possessive guy simply learns a lesson about toxic masculinity. It also could have accidentally drifted into ugly territory where the film frames the woman as somehow deserving punishment for "friend-zoning" him, as if she had been manipulating his feelings all along. But what's fascinating is that the movie avoids both traps almost entirely. This film works because of how emotionally complicated the relationships actually are. When he makes the wish, the realization slowly creeps in that he probably never needed to make it in the first place. The film subtly suggests that there may have already been a real path toward intimacy between them if he had simply been direct, vulnerable, and emotionally honest. Instead, what he wants is a shortcut. That's what the wish really represents. It's not the creation of love, but the desire to bypass the difficult, awkward, human process of earning closeness naturally. That idea feels deeply connected to modern dating culture. So much of contemporary romance now revolves around avoiding vulnerability altogether. Instead of slowly getting to know someone, people jump immediately toward manipulation, performance, sexual escalation, or emotional pressure. The film understands that impulse in a way that feels painfully current. He doesn't use the wish because he has no path toward connection. He uses it because he is unwilling to risk the uncertainty and discomfort that real connection requires. What makes this even more interesting is that their friendship before everything collapses is actually good. There is real affection between them. The movie never portrays her as somebody cynically keeping him around for validation while waiting for a "better" guy. Their relationship has warmth, humor, and genuine intimacy already built into it. Which is why the horror of the film becomes larger than simply "obsession." The tragedy is that he destroys something meaningful because he cannot accept allowing it to evolve naturally. And that's why the film has just dug into my mind and won't let go. Underneath all the supernatural insanity, the film is really about what happens when people try to force relationships into forms they were never meant to take. Once coercion enters the equation, even subtly, people stop becoming authentic versions of themselves. The relationship itself becomes poisoned. The film understands that emotional manipulation exists far beyond magic wishes or horror-movie extremes. There are countless real-world ways people pressure, corner, guilt, or psychologically steer relationships into existence, and the movie taps into that discomfort without ever reducing itself to a simple moral lecture. This is why Obsession feels more mature than it has any right to. It understands something many films ignore: sometimes a truly meaningful friendship is already a profound form of love. And sometimes the inability to recognize that can destroy the very thing you were trying to hold onto in the first place.
label: cinematic_critique

text: witnessing inde navarrette's performance in this is how i imagine it felt to watch isabelle adjani's subway scene in possession upon its premiere. you feel like you're getting in on the ground floor of something truly special, something that will be studied and celebrated for decades to come - both for the performance and performer. so often the industry has to play "catch-up" with the most revered performances in genre films, rarely giving them their due in the moment and only celebrating the towering talents of their performer when they flex them in more "conventional" fare. the recent widespread embrace of work like demi moore's in the substance and amy madigan's in weapons gives me hope navarrette won't suffer a similar fate, but then again, unlike those two, navarrette is a "new face" easier to overlook until she's made "more of a name for herself" a decade or so down the line. but in my opinion, it might be even more impressive for a relative unknown to break out of near obscurity with a single role that holds its own against some of the best the genre has ever seen. and sure, awards aren't everything. this is a performance that'll endure whether it's honored that way or not. but wouldn't it be cool for them to be ahead of the curve for once? great art doesn't always need time to age into its greatness. sometimes, that's just how it arrives.

text: A man's complete blind spot to a woman's personhood and interiority taken to cruel extremes — fun to pair with The Drama. Rewatching this movie, I think I found the kernel that separates it from its YouTube-to-cinema contemporaries: Rather than perpetuating shallow "isn't it all so fucked up" provocations, Obsession sinks its audience into a gnawing discomfort. There's a tightrope here that Curry Barker walks, and he wobbles to either get a laugh or a wince, but he never lets you forget that everything Bear does is violatingly evil. Obviously everyone is giving Inde Navarrette her due flowers for all the unbelievable gags, but the scenes where Nikki's agency peeks through are some of the most upsetting things you'll see all year.

text: At the screening, I was wearing the same sweater as Bear (Michael Johnston) wears in the movie. That adds an extra level of cringe to the viewing experience. Bear was hoping Nikki (Inde Navarrette does a lot of the heavy lifting here) and him could be more than friends. I was hoping to look good in my sweater. As Bear, I can make all the One Willow Wishes in the world, my hopes will never come true, not really. Even a movie star like Michael Johnston doesn't look good in it. What were you thinking, crazy old man? Nikki will never really love Bear. Not in that way. When forced into obsession, an almost naked Nikki wears nothing but that sweater to watch Bear sleep. The sweater smells like him. It is a very soft and cosy sweater. It feels so good on your naked skin. And mine smells great too. Unfortunately for Bear, Nikki does some even weirder shit than that. As the movie progresses it moves from cringe to uncomfortable funny. Funny for us, not so funny for Bear, Ian (Cooper Tomlinson), Sarah (Megan Lawless) and the tragic Nikki. As far as romantic horror comedies go, this is a pretty dark one. I am with the hype. If it wasn't a hype already, I am now officially declaring it one. Before jumping on the hype train, check if you are okay with male anxiety and female hysteria.

text: With peace and love, I have no idea what's going on with the love for this movie. It's... really bad? On a writing and directing level, this movie feels like it was made by high schoolers. Everything is so amateurishly cobbled together and scattershot, to a point where it's almost shocking it was released like this. Decisions as simple as where the camera is, when a cut is made, or where a scene starts or ends so regularly feel unintentionally bizarre and ill-fitting. It mistakes being LOUD for being effective, when so often all it has to lean back on is "oh my god isn't she SO CRAZY?!?!" The sad thing is that there's actually some decent threads that could be pulled on and followed here. When it's most effective is the moments where we see Nikki's actual consciousness trying breaking through. Credit to Inde Navarrette who, let's be clear, is phenomenal across this whole movie, and not only far and away the best part of it, but also the only reason why anything about it that works does work at all. The shimmers where we're able to get windows into her perspective are harrowing, but that never really feels like the perspective through which this film was envisioned, and the lens it was built from isn't even dug into beyond the most rudimentary observations. There's nothing remotely terrifying about the litany of "oh she's so weird" shallow monotony Barker employs, nor the irritatingly vacuous shock value being hammered in. But what is genuinely unnerving is when we're forced to reckon with the real human being who is lost and confused and afraid in there. Who has no idea what's happening to her, and at a certain point is simply begging to be put out of her misery. That's where the human toll of this story comes into play, and Navarrette really raises that above what's on the page to make it land. On the counter, the entirety of what's going on with Bear is kind of a disaster. We never get much of a sense of the inner life of this guy. Not before the wish, but also not even after. Scenes with him just drone on with him giving the same sort of blank or freaked out expression, but it feels torturous wanting to know more about what he's actually thinking or feeling beyond "this is so weird." Unfortunately that seems to be where the script's insights really end. And while Navarrette is able to elevate the material, it's the opposite for Michael Johnston, who feels like he's drowning trying to make even the smallest gesture or line of dialogue work here. There's a moment where Johnston's character asks Nikki, "What would be so wrong with being with me?," which for maybe the first time felt like a mildly intriguing look into his psyche. It was horrifying to hear after everything he had seen that this was the first question he would think to ask Nikki in this specific moment, and I wanted to scream, "Yes, let's dig into that!" but two seconds later we were sitting in a car waiting for the next big obvious jump scare. I don't know, I found this whole thing miserably tedious, with its few bright spots only serving to further frustrate things. Inde Navarrette very much innocent though, as she delivers one of the best horror performances in recent years.
label: cinematic_critique

text: What the film has going for it is Inde Navarrette's performance. It is bold, inventive, biting, and revelatory in all the ways the film itself can only dream of embodying. As the film lurches to its obvious, mean-spirited conclusion, it is evident Nikki is merely a vehicle for shocking moments of brutal violence, gross-out dark humor, and male fears of women who are just too much. But Navarrette gives a stunning performance that clearly delineates between real-Nikki's fear and whatever entity is housed in her body's malevolent manic pixie nightmare act. She brings such remarkable detail to a character that is ultimately merely a tool for the film's middling scares and lack of imagination. It isn't a bad movie overall, I was entertained. But mostly I was frustrated. There's a damning scene that touches on the wounds Barker lacks the curiosity and ingenuity to explore. Bear gets urgent texts from Sarah to meet immediately. He slips from the bed when another moment in which the real Nikki breaks through occurs. She speaks directly to him. "Bear, please kill me," she repeatedly pleads. She mentions that "she", as in the entity housed in Nikki's body, is asleep. She begs to be freed from this hellish prison. "Is it so bad being with me?" Bear asks. "I've never been with you, Bear," Nikki responds. This is a moment of remarkable cowardice — not just Bear's refusal to look at the horror his actions have engendered, but the cowardice of the filmmaker himself. Every time these characters have had sex Nikki is essentially being raped. The movie is so eager to frame Bear as a victim, it loses out on a far richer line of inquiry and muddles its own story.
label: cinematic_critique

text: Part of what makes Obsession so unpredictable is its tendency to keep the scales of comedy and horror unbalanced. Here is a movie that will swing wildly from laugh out loud sight gags and absurdist line-reads to a deeply disturbing image at any moment's notice. The film is built on this uneasy tension, and it keeps the viewer on their toes. As an examination of the 'nice guy' and the 'dream girl', consent culture, coercion etc - it's not a slam dunk. But as a thrilling night out at the multiplex? Succeeds with flying colors. Look out for Inde Navarrette's performance here. Truly remarkable. She's the vital source of the film's wicked and ravenous energy.

Once you start noticing how horror directors treat women and their bodies vs men's you will never stop seeing it.

text: If I'm being honest, it can get a bit tiring to bring up Hereditary every time a new horror movie drops that feels even slightly ambitious. After watching Obsession, I'm starting to wonder if we're on the cusp of another shift. If you had told me this was Curry Barker's feature debut, I wouldn't have believed you. The guy moves like a veteran. There were several moments where I was legitimately stressed out because I genuinely had no idea what was coming next, especially with Inde Navarrette's character. She sinks her teeth into this role and doesn't let go. This is one of those movies that demands more than one viewing. If Curry Barker keeps making movies at this level, he's going to be a household name in the horror genre for years to come.

text: Not sure why I want to go to bat for this one, especially when a lot of people I like and respect seem intent on taking a bat to it. I've now seen Obsession twice and think it to be a rich text. I'm compelled by the filmmaker's decision to never let us see Nikki except through Bear's eyes. Bear is mostly projecting loneliness and need onto another party, which sets the tone for what is pretty obviously a movie about a pernicious form of nice-guy entitlement. Nikki is a cipher precisely because Bear doesn't really know her, which gives the moments where her submerged consciousness suddenly emerges a little bit of friction. That question — if it really would have been so bad to have been with him — comes from a dark place, and however Barker has accessed it, I've been affected by it two times now.

text: One of the worst downstream effects of Wolf of Wall Street being basically the most important and influential movie of the 21st century is that its structure and moralizing has become the fall-back for basically any story. Obsession is a WoWS-like. It is a movie where we see a thing happen, but only on the periphery. An insecure man accidentally robs a woman of her bodily autonomy to a supernatural degree, creating a misogynistic caricature that is ironically ruining his life, so he just kills himself to escape any culpability. I like a mean movie, and this is mean, occasionally to great effect. But it is sloppy and uncommitted in ways I found really disappointing. Fundamentally, the movie is not prepared to give incels their fantasy and it does a really bad job of it. It would be more effective to be clear, but Barker is 26 and this is his first movie and he doesn't know how to write a woman speaking normally. You can point out how we never truly see Nikki, how he reveals pathetic sadistic entitlement when she's asleep, how much of the film is filtered through his eyes. But it's not really prepared to suggest that using DARVO tactics might make Bear feel really good. It is not confident enough to really commit to being evil enough to sell the depths of its depraved roundabout empathy.
label: cinematic_critique

text: so for my 10th watch i thought i'd do a full write up as there's so much i want to say about this very special movie. obsession has had such a profound effect on me and i want to give it the appreciation and discussion it deserves. yes, obsession is terrifying and does an incredible job of being a horror movie. but there's so much to dissect. starting with themes of agency and autonomy, nikki quite literally has these stripped away from her, which by itself is absolutely heartbreaking. curry barker stated it's essentially a sunken place situation, where nikki sees and hears everything but is in the passenger seat, unable to take control. the bedroom scene is a big one, where bear rapes nikki, who is stone-faced crying (the real nikki) but making sounds (entity nikki). what makes this even more upsetting is pre-wish nikki is a genuinely good person with big hopes and aspirations. there's this whole discourse on social media on whether bear is a villain and i honestly can't even comprehend how that's not obvious — he is not only the bad guy, but completely subhuman. the ending is incredibly bleak and upsetting, but i truly feel the original darker ending would've been too much. this is why obsession will remain my number one movie for the rest of the year. a very, very special movie.
label: cinematic_critique

text: first of all, i feel like i need to address this before going any further because unfortunately, everyone always overhypes everything. DISCLAIMER: no, this is not the greatest horror film of the century. this is not elevated horror. this is campy horror, and will be judged as such. now with that being said, it was really good. what makes the film work as well as it does is Inde Navarette. what a monstrous talent! the amount of subtle emotional shifts she's able to pull off from one second to the next is insanity. it never becomes over the top. the other thing that really works is baker's ability to constantly blur fear and laughter together. he's not scaring you in the traditional sense. he's making you uncomfortable, conflicted, embarrassed, sometimes all at once. but the film definitely has flaws. the secondary characters are weak, some of the dialogue doesn't land, and visually it feels safe and conventional. but what i found genuinely interesting is the subtext: the film quietly understands from beginning to end that he is never the victim. it's about the desire for control, emotional cowardice, and the selfish male ego.
label: cinematic_critique

text: Details I only noticed after several rewatches: Nikki wasn't possessed by some entity from "One Wish Willow," but rather she's a version of Bear representing his obsession. After Bear makes the wish, Nikki says "my cat died," then corrects herself, saying it was Bear's cat, but she was referring to herself as if she were Bear. So she's just a version of Bear and not just any entity. Another explanation is in the photos with Bear's lunch where it says "it's you, not me." This was the real Nikki trying to say that everything happening was Bear's version controlling her. So all the obsession is actually Bear's. And if Nikki really is a version of Bear, then it means that Bear doesn't love anyone, he loves himself. "One Wish Willow" just makes those obsessions extravagant.
label: cinematic_critique

text: This being the biggest pickup at TIFF by a distributor makes a lot of sense. Everything about its high-concept cringey teen rom-com turned gory possession movie and sense of style is readymade to slide alongside the Philippous or Creggar. On one level that did kind of give this a bit of a ceiling for me, just how obviously it's chasing its own commercial packaging aspirations. But on the other, how this goes about stretching such a one-note premise to nearly 2 hours and keeps it constantly moving and escalating was pretty impressive. My favorite details were all the horrific ones about what it might look and feel like to lose autonomy over your body — the scream on the phone call, the begging for death in sleep bit, the ending. Shoutout to Inde Navarrette who this is basically a showcase for. Every little stare, smile, disgusting bit of violence or body horror has a manic fixation to it that just keeps getting more and more intense until its breaking point.
label: cinematic_critique

text: Sue me: I like going to the AMC, eating popcorn, and chatting with my friends about new movies. But I don't like it this much. The genre has gone to a place that not even critical interest can carry me, so, unless there is a really good reason to do so, I will not be attending these movies anymore. At the very least not the ones directed by YouTubers. Nearly every shot in this film is center-framed with ample headspace. The content is almost irrelevant when the formal means through which that content is expressed are so bankrupt and hacky. For horror movies about the repression of female rage in patriarchal romance, please refer to Sisters, Audition, and Gone Girl. Those movies offer layered, intricate, and provocative explorations of the theme in question. I really don't understand why anyone would accord a movie like Obsession the same level of esteem as those films.
label: cinematic_critique

text: My man is so damn indecisive from minute one that it became genuinely stressful. I wanted to punch him in the face. The hype surrounding this movie has been unreal, and that can always go one of two ways. Luckily, this one firmly falls into the first category. Curry Barker does a masterful job building tension almost immediately. I'll admit I was a little skeptical after hearing all the overwhelming praise Inde Navarrette was getting, especially the Oscar talk. But as the film progressed she completely won me over. Her performance becomes increasingly unhinged while still feeling painfully human. All in all, this is horror executed in a way that feels genuinely stressful and deeply uncomfortable. I can't remember the last time a horror film made me feel this consistently uneasy. If Curry Barker keeps delivering work at this level, he's got an incredibly promising career ahead of him.
label: cinematic_critique

text: Not to be contrarian (being contrarian), but the near unanimous praise for this movie suggests people are projecting their own ideas onto it, rather than engaging with what's actually there. Because Obsession is not a film of ideas. It does not explore consent, coercion, toxic masculinity, male violence or any of the other myriad gender-politics themes its hooky premise gestures towards. The film is too afraid to present its woman-enslaving protagonist as the villain he is — or even recognise Nikki's basic humanity — so what we're left with is escalating, gory violence solely exacted against the female characters. Cowardly, misogynistic, callow.
label: cinematic_critique

text: Obsession casts a spell and barely lets you blink. The acting is surprisingly good in this movie. But Inde Navarrette is the reason this thing works as well as it does. Her character erratically switches between different psyches and moods at any given moment, and Inde flips between them like it's nothing. The lighting work deserves a shoutout too… there's a choice to silhouette Nikki whenever she's deep in her possessed state. What I loved most is what the movie doesn't explain. Bear googling the Willow, scrolling through forums for reviews like it's a sketchy Instagram ad… that's exactly how I'd react. And that phone call scene? When the customer service guy asks if Bear wants to talk to Nikki... but you hear her screaming in the background... my jaw hit the floor.
label: cinematic_critique

text: I sat down with Curry Barker ahead of the release of his very scary second feature, Obsession. He wanted Nikki to feel like a really heightened, relatable thing — not a demon-possessed woman, but a crazy jealous girlfriend. That note he gave Inde Navarrette explains so much of what makes the performance work.
label: cinematic_critique

text: The rhythm of this thing makes it feel so squirmy and alive. Every nightmarish scare and every dreamy wish is scored by this thrumming, hungry synth score and each of these moments is snapped in two by a hard cut to silence at the most abrupt time. These auditory cuts come consistently throughout the film, yanking us out of the film's spell again and again. It's like the film is screaming at us. Snap out of it! Look at what's become of these people! Nothing here would be half as good if it weren't for Inde Navarrette. The scares, the jokes, everything in between hinges on her ability to commit and shoulder almost the full weight of the film. And she does so with ease, like she was born to do it. Her work here is genuinely, truly, deeply terrifying. Obsession is nasty. It's mean. It's shockingly cruel. It very clearly revels in making its puppy dog eyed protagonist suffer for his actions as much as possible.
label: cinematic_critique

text: "I'll be anything you want me to be." It has become exceedingly difficult for a horror film to have an effect on me or get under my skin. I was genuinely surprised by how much Obsession unsettled me. This film is going to launch the careers of all of its cast members and director Curry Barker in a huge way. Inde Navarrette is going to blow the fuck up fairly quickly because of this performance. Nikki has to feel natural and grounded while also conveying warmth and comfortability. As the possession takes over, she has to turn the tide gradually, yet not quite switch into "horror mode." The tonal shifts just in her performance are impressive. Her movements are absolutely horrifying whether she's watching Bear sleep or just doing the creepiest backwards walk I've ever seen. I think Barker nailed the ending perfectly with him killing himself and giving her agency back to her.
label: cinematic_critique

text: I found this thrilling, hilarious, and incredibly fucked up. Curry Barker has a firm grasp of horror comedy and genre. Inde Navarrette gives one of my favorite performances of the year and honestly the decade here. I love how much humor it pulls from its deranged premise, but most of all, I love how gnarly this is. text: this pretty viscerally upset me in ways that few movies ever have, and I've still been feeling the panic and anxiety in my body the next day. overall, I think that's a compliment; this is a truly frightening experience in a way that few horror films have ever been for me. still, I'm left wrestling with the emotional takeaway of the film. the film clearly reads as a commentary on young men who see a woman's love as fundamentally owed to them by birthright. looking down at the rows in front of me during my screening, I could see several groups of teenage boys — many of whom pointed and laughed at various things between each other throughout. when the credits rolled, I couldn't help but ask myself: are those boys going to walk away thinking "I should be more careful about treating women as though they owe me their love"? Or are they merely going to say "bro, it would be scary to have a girlfriend like that"? I don't know. I can't help but wonder what a woman would have done differently with similar material.
label: cinematic_critique

text: I tried to see this at multiple film festivals over the past year and never could. The screenings were packed, demand was through the roof, and everyone walking out was buzzing. Sometimes that kind of hype is a warning sign. But then the wider critical response came in just as strong, audiences loved it on release, and it landed an A minus CinemaScore. Horror movies virtually never score above a B+. So my expectations were high going in. It earned every bit of it. Obsession is a genuinely intelligent spin on the wish fulfillment subgenre. What makes it so effective is that the horror does not come from cheap tricks. It comes from helplessness. The lead actress is a revelation here. One of my favorite films of the year.
label: cinematic_critique

text: Obsession is the horror movie of the moment, and there's nothing to look at in it. Curry Barker and cinematographer Taylor Clemons have two visual ideas that they repeatedly opt for: framing Nikki in heavy shadows or silhouette, and the almost constant usage of underlit shallow-depth of field close-ups foregrounding Bear. These two visual ideas are coherent within the tableaux of the story, but just because it is logical does not mean that it is exciting or worthwhile to watch. Obsession feels ready made to be scraped for algorithmic scrolling on the apps without losing any visual information, because there is not much to lose in the first place.
label: cinematic_critique

text: There's not a lot happening here, so I don't have a lot to say. There's no true curiosity on display here; no sense of awareness from writer/director Curry Barker; and not an iota of attention to its inherent gendered or sexual politics, much less how they relate to consent. The one moment where Nikki has a flicker of lucidity and begs for Bear to kill her either needed to be the entire point of the film or not be included at all. Obsession is such a glib, half-baked project. It's not just that each woman exists as a mouthpiece to further the obvious narrative conflicts, although that is a major issue. This movie as it exists is the first half of a movie stretched out to feature length. Tell it from Nikki's perspective, have Bear kill himself at the midpoint, and have Nikki be free of the wish but with a shattered psyche for the second half. Inde Navarrette is good with what she's given. I hope someone gives her more than a few crumbs to work with next time.
label: cinematic_critique

The whole film sits on this weird, queasy wavelength where something always feels wrong even during the nothing moments. The film keeps blurring the line between affection and possession. Even the framing and cinematography is infected by that idea. The camera is so fixated on them, trapping them inside the frame. On paper it's a tiny film with a modest budget, but it never feels small. And Inde Navarrette... MY GAWD. She somehow makes you smile in discomfort while simultaneously making your skin crawl. Every smile of hers feels disarmingly warm and vaguely sinister at the same time. She gave me the same feeling I got watching Isabella Adjani in Possession. Just pure unpredictability.

text: One of the wildest experiences I've ever had in a movie theater. My darling was gripping my arm so tightly that it went completely numb. Curry Barker captures the essence of contemporary horror and wraps it in a concept so absorbing and flexible, enhanced by sound design and lighting work so perfect it's worthy of applause. Inde Navarrette terrified me like never before; her face sometimes looked digitized, as if she were genuinely possessed during filming. Her expressions and gazes remain frozen in my mind, like an obsession. My favorite performance of the year so far, without a doubt.
